# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Physical Characteristics of Fruits and Vegetables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Physical characteristics* of fruits and vegetables refer to the measurable geometric, gravimetric, optical, and mechanical properties that define the external and internal morphology of plant produce. These include **size, shape, weight, volume, density, specific gravity, surface area, porosity, and color**. They are critical for grading, sorting, packaging design, and process equipment sizing in food processing industries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Baked Apple" Picture:**
> Imagine two apples on a kitchen counter. One is small, round, and dense; the other is large, irregular, and puffy. Even before tasting, you can already tell they will behave *very differently* in a fruit-pie factory. The smaller one will fit neatly into the sorting cups, the larger one will take longer to dry, and the softer one will bruise more easily during transport. This is exactly why food engineers *measure* and *record* physical characteristics — they determine **how the produce will behave in every machine it touches**, from the washing tank to the packaging line.

### 1.1.1 Geometric Properties (Size & Shape)

- **Size:** Defined by characteristic dimensions — *length (L)*, *width (W)*, and *thickness (T)* — usually measured in **millimeters (mm)** or **centimeters (cm)** using a **vernier caliper** or **image analysis system**.
- **Shape:** Often expressed using dimensionless indices:
  - **Aspect Ratio (AR) = L / W**
  - **Sphericity (Φ)** — a measure of how close the object is to a perfect sphere (Φ = 1 for a perfect sphere, Φ < 1 for elongated or irregular shapes).
  - **Roundness / Oblateness** — used for tomatoes, apples, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Shape determines the **angle of repose** (which controls flow in hoppers and conveyors) and the **drag coefficient** during air classification or water flotation sorting.

### 1.1.2 Gravimetric Properties (Mass, Volume, Density)

- **Mass (m):** Measured in **grams (g)** or **kilograms (kg)** using electronic balances.
- **Volume (V):** Determined by **water displacement method** (Archimedes' principle) or by **gas (air) pycnometer** for irregular shapes.
- **Bulk Density (ρ_b):** $\rho_b = \dfrac{m_{bulk}}{V_{bulk}}$ — mass per unit volume of a bulk-stacked sample.
- **True Density (ρ_t):** Density of the solid material excluding inter-particle voids.
- **Specific Gravity (SG):** $\text{SG} = \dfrac{\rho_{produce}}{\rho_{water}}$ at 4 °C.
- **Porosity (ε):** $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$ — fraction of void space in a bulk sample.

### 1.1.3 Optical Properties (Color & Appearance)

- **Color** is the *most important* sensory attribute for consumer acceptance.
- Quantified using colorimeters / spectrophotometers in the **CIELAB (L*, a*, b*)** color space.
  - **L*** = Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white)
  - **a*** = Redness (+) to Greenness (−)
  - **b*** = Yellowness (+) to Blueness (−)
- **Total Color Difference:** $\Delta E = \sqrt{(L_2^* - L_1^*)^2 + (a_2^* - a_1^*)^2 + (b_2^* - b_1^*)^2}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Color Matters in Processing:** Color indicates *chlorophyll degradation* (degreening), *carotenoid synthesis* (ripening), and *enzymatic browning* (polyphenol oxidase activity). It is monitored for **HTST, UHT, and aseptic processing** quality control.

---

## 1.2 Textural Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Texture* of a fruit or vegetable is the **rheological and structural response of the produce to applied mechanical force**. It is a *multi-parameter* attribute encompassing **hardness, firmness, cohesiveness, adhesiveness, springiness, chewiness, gumminess, and juiciness**, and is governed by the cell wall composition, turgor pressure, and intercellular bonding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Mango Touch Test":**
> Pick up a raw mango — it is rock hard. Press your thumb in — nothing happens. Wait a few days; the same mango now yields slightly to gentle pressure, signalling ripeness. A few days later, it is soft and pulpy. **The same fruit, at three different times, has three completely different textures.** Food engineers measure this *objectively* using devices like the **Texture Profile Analyzer (TPA)** and the **Magness-Taylor (MT) firmness tester** to remove human subjectivity from ripeness judgement.

### 1.2.1 Instrumental Texture Measurement

- **Puncture Test (Magness-Taylor):** A plunger of standard diameter (commonly **7.94 mm = 5/16 inch**) is pushed into the flesh after peel removal; the **peak force (in Newtons or kgf)** at fruit rupture is recorded.
- **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA):** A double-compression test (Bourne, 1968) that mimics the two-bite chewing action and yields **7 texture parameters** simultaneously.
- **Kramer Shear Cell:** Multi-blade shear test used for bulk produce like peas, cut beans.
- **Penetrometer:** Measures resistance to penetration, widely used for mango, papaya, avocado.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The standard **Magness-Taylor probe diameter is 7.94 mm (5/16 inch)** — this is a **frequently asked 2-mark question** in KTU exams.

### 1.2.2 Common Textural Defects in Processed Produce

| Defect | Cause | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Mealiness** | Breakdown of pectin → cell separation | Detrimental to canned peaches, pears |
| **Woolliness** | Cold storage injury (chilling) | Affects peach, nectarine quality |
| **Lignification** | Excess lignin deposition | Common in over-mature carrots, asparagus |
| **Softening** | Enzymatic pectin degradation | Major issue in tomato paste, guava pulp |
| **Hard-core** | Tissue lignification during storage | Common in over-ripe apples |

---

## 1.3 Structure and Composition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Structure* refers to the **hierarchical organization of plant tissues** (cellular → tissue → organ level), while *composition* denotes the **biochemical constituents** (water, carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, vitamins, minerals, pigments, and organic acids) that determine nutritional value, processing behavior, and shelf life of fruits and vegetables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Building and the Recipe":**
> *Structure* is the **architectural blueprint** of a fruit — the way its cells, walls, and tissues are organized. *Composition* is the **recipe of ingredients** (water, sugar, acid, fibre) packed inside that building. A baker needs **both**: the building's shape determines what oven racks it fits on (process equipment), and the recipe determines how it will bake (heat & mass transfer during processing).

### 1.3.1 Cellular Structure of Plant Tissues

The fruit/vegetable is a complex multi-cellular system. Three main tissue types govern texture:

- **Parenchyma** — thin-walled, large vacuolated cells; forms the **bulk of soft fruits**. Its breakdown causes softening.
- **Collenchyma** — unevenly thickened living cells; provides **flexible support** in stems, young leaves.
- **Sclerenchyma** — thick lignified walls; provides **rigid support**. Excess causes *grittiness* in pear, *hardness* in over-mature beans.

### 1.3.2 Cell Wall Architecture (Critical for Texture)

The primary cell wall is a composite of:
- **Cellulose** — load-bearing microfibrils (β-1,4-glucan chains)
- **Hemicellulose** (Xyloglucan, Xylan, Mannan) — cross-links cellulose microfibrils
- **Pectin** (Protopectin → Pectin → Pectic acid) — the **"cement"** that holds cells together
- **Lignin** — phenolic polymer; rigidity in mature/woody tissues
- **Structural Proteins** (Extensins) — minor role

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept — Pectin Chemistry:**
> Protopectin (insoluble, in unripe fruit) → Pectin (soluble, in ripe fruit) → Pectic acid (de-esterified, in over-ripe/soft fruit). **This transition is the biochemical basis of fruit softening** during ripening and is enzymatically controlled by **pectin methylesterase (PME)** and **polygalacturonase (PG)**.

### 1.3.3 Proximate Composition of Fruits & Vegetables

| Component | Typical Range (Fresh Weight Basis) | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Water** | 70–95 % | Determines *drying energy*, *freezing load*, *juice yield* |
| **Carbohydrates** (sugars, starch, fibre) | 2–25 % | Sweetness, fermentation substrate, viscosity |
| **Proteins** | 0.5–5 % | Nutritional; low compared to animal foods |
| **Lipids** | 0.1–1 % (fruits); up to 50 % (avocado) | Affects *oil extraction* and *texture* |
| **Organic Acids** (citric, malic, tartaric) | 0.2–2 % | pH, flavor, preservation |
| **Vitamins & Pigments** | trace | Nutritional; colour control in processing |
| **Minerals (ash)** | 0.3–2 % | Nutritional |

---

## 1.4 Maturity Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Maturity* of a fruit or vegetable is the **state of physiological development at which a plant organ reaches a stage suitable for a designated end-use** (fresh consumption, processing, storage, or long-distance transport). *Maturity standards* are **measurable indices** (physical, chemical, physiological, or subjective) used to objectively determine whether a produce has reached the desired maturity.

### 1.4.1 Two Critical Concepts (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction students often confuse in exams:**

- **Physiological Maturity:** The stage when the plant organ has completed its natural growth and development; seeds are capable of germination. *Example: Mature-green tomato is physiologically mature but not ripe.*
- **Horticultural / Commercial Maturity:** The stage when the produce has reached the **qualities desired by the consumer or processor** (right size, colour, firmness, flavor). *Example: A banana is horticulturally mature when yellow, even if harvested green earlier.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Climacteric vs Non-Climacteric Fruits** — directly linked to maturity standards:
> - **Climacteric** (banana, mango, tomato, apple, papaya): Ripen *after* harvest, with a respiratory burst and ethylene surge.
> - **Non-climacteric** (citrus, grapes, strawberry, pineapple): Ripen *only* on the plant; no post-harvest ripening.

### 1.4.2 Classification of Maturity Indices

| Category | Examples of Indices |
|---|---|
| **Physical / Visual** | Size, shape, colour, surface morphology, abscission, firmness |
| **Chemical** | TSS (°Brix), titratable acidity, TSS/Acid ratio, pH, starch content, sugar content |
| **Physiological** | Respiration rate, ethylene evolution, internal ethylene concentration |
| **Calculated** | Heat units (Growing Degree Days), days from full bloom |
| **Subjective** | Touch, sound (watermelon thump), aroma |

### 1.4.3 Maturity Indices for Selected Fruits/Vegetables

| Produce | Index Used | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Banana** | Peel colour (von Loesecke chart) | Stage 3–4 (green to green-trace-yellow) for shipping |
| **Mango** | Specific gravity; peel colour | SG = 1.00–1.02; flesh TSS ≥ 14 °Brix |
| **Apple** | Starch-iodine test; firmness | Firmness = 65–75 N (for 'Red Delicious') |
| **Tomato** | External colour (USDA chart) | Mature-green to pink stage |
| **Pineapple** | Eye flatness; shell colour | TSS ≥ 12 %; yellow eye base |
| **Watermelon** | Tendril drying, thump test, ground-spot colour | Dull, hollow sound |
| **Potato** | Specific gravity; haulm death | SG > 1.08 for processing |
| **Grape** | TSS / acid ratio | TSS ≥ 16 °Brix, ratio 20:1 |
| **Citrus** | TSS/Acid ratio, juice % | Ratio 8:1 to 12:1, juice ≥ 30 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in processing:** Harvesting at correct maturity determines the **storage life, quality of processed products, and yield** (juice yield, pulp yield, etc.). Wrong maturity = low yield + poor quality + high rejection rate at the factory.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Characteristics — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Sphericity (Φ) — Derivation Logic

**Sphericity** is the ratio of the **surface area of a sphere having the same volume as the object** to the **actual surface area of the object**.

$$\Phi = \dfrac{A_s}{A_p}$$

where:
- $A_s$ = surface area of a sphere with the same volume as the particle
- $A_p$ = actual surface area of the particle

**For an agricultural produce of three principal dimensions (L, W, T):**

$$\Phi = \dfrac{(L \cdot W \cdot T)^{1/3}}{L}$$

A perfectly spherical produce (like a cherry tomato) has $\Phi = 1$; an elongated carrot has $\Phi \approx 0.5$.

### 2.1.2 Bulk Density vs True Density

- **True Density ($\rho_t$):** Density of the *solid material itself* without voids. Measured by **pycnometer** (gas or liquid).
- **Bulk Density ($\rho_b$):** Mass per unit *bulk volume* including inter-particle void spaces.
- **Particle Density ($\rho_p$):** Density of an *individual* particle including internal pores.
- **Porosity ($\varepsilon$):** $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Bulk density determines **storage bin volume**, **package size**, and **transportation weight/volume efficiency**.

### 2.1.3 Optical Theory — CIELAB Color Measurement

The **L\*, a\*, b\*** system is derived from the CIE XYZ tristimulus values. The transformation is non-linear but for most food applications, the **Hue angle** and **Chroma** are computed:

$$\text{Hue angle } (h^\circ) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{b^*}{a^*}\right)$$

$$\text{Chroma } (C^*) = \sqrt{(a^*)^2 + (b^*)^2}$$

These two parameters describe *what the color is* and *how saturated it is*, respectively.

---

## 2.2 Textural Characteristics — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 The Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) — Bourne (1968)

A TPA curve from a two-bite compression test on a cylindrical sample yields **seven** textural parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardness** | $H$ | Peak force of first compression |
| **Cohesiveness** | $C$ | Ratio of positive force area of 2nd / 1st bite |
| **Adhesiveness** | $A$ | Negative force area of first bite |
| **Springiness** | $S$ | Distance recovered between end of 1st and start of 2nd bite |
| **Cohesiveness × Springiness** | — | (no standard name) |
| **Gumminess** | $G$ | $H \times C$ |
| **Chewiness** | $C_h$ | $H \times C \times S$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams:** "**H**ard **C**ohesive **A**dhesive **S**pringy **G**ummy **C**hewy"

### 2.2.2 Rheological Classification of Plant Tissues

Plant tissues behave as **viscoelastic** materials — combining elastic (solid-like) and viscous (fluid-like) responses. The **Maxwell model** and **Kelvin–Voigt model** are applied to describe creep and stress relaxation:

$$\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon(t) + \eta \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} \quad \text{(Kelvin-Voigt)}$$

$$\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\tau} = E \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} \quad \text{(Maxwell, with } \tau = \eta/E \text{)}$$

where $E$ = elastic modulus, $\eta$ = viscosity, $\tau$ = relaxation time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** During *thermal processing* (blanching, pasteurization), plant tissues undergo **stress relaxation**. The texture changes (softening) are quantitatively predicted using these viscoelastic models.

---

## 2.3 Structure & Composition — Biochemical Drivers

### 2.3.1 The Pectin Pathway (Maturity & Texture Engine)

$$\text{Protopectin} \xrightarrow{\text{PME}} \text{Pectin} \xrightarrow{\text{PG}} \text{Pectic Acid} \xrightarrow{\text{Pectate lyase}} \text{Softening}$$

- **PME (Pectin Methylesterase):** Removes methyl groups → free carboxyl groups.
- **PG (Polygalacturonase):** Hydrolyses α-1,4 glycosidic bonds in pectin chain.
- **Pectate Lyase:** Cleaves glycosidic bonds via β-elimination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Control:** In tomato paste manufacturing, the natural ripening-induced PG activity is *enhanced* by hot-break treatment (85–95 °C) which inactivates PG-inhibitors, accelerating pectin breakdown and increasing paste viscosity.

### 2.3.2 Respiratory Climacteric — Ethylene Trigger

For climacteric fruits, maturity is marked by a **respiratory peak** with **autocatalytic ethylene production**:

$$\text{Methionine} \xrightarrow{\text{ACC synthase}} \text{ACC} \xrightarrow{\text{ACC oxidase}} \text{Ethylene} + \text{CO}_2$$

- **ACC** = 1-Aminocyclopropane-1-Carboxylic Acid
- **Ethylene threshold for ripening** ≈ **0.1–1.0 ppm** for most climacteric fruits.
- **1-MCP (1-Methylcyclopropene)** is an ethylene receptor blocker used commercially to delay ripening.

---

## 2.4 Maturity Standards — Mathematical Foundations

### 2.4.1 Total Soluble Solids (TSS / °Brix)

Measured by **refractometer**. 1 °Brix ≈ 1 % sucrose w/w solution.

$$\text{TSS (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{mass of soluble solids}}{\text{mass of total solution}} \times 100$$

### 2.4.2 Titratable Acidity (TA)

$$\text{TA (\%)} = \dfrac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times \text{meq factor} \times 100}{V_{sample}}$$

The **TSS/Acid Ratio** is the dominant flavor index in citrus, mango, pineapple.

### 2.4.3 Heat Unit / Growing Degree Day (GDD)

$$GDD = \sum \dfrac{T_{max} + T_{min}}{2} - T_{base}$$

A crop is considered mature when cumulative GDD reaches a crop-specific threshold (e.g., grapes require **1500–2000 GDD** for wine maturity).

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sphericity of a Mango

**Problem:** A mango has principal dimensions L = 12 cm, W = 8 cm, T = 7 cm. Determine its sphericity and comment on the engineering implication.

**Step 1: Identify the dimensions.**
- Longest intercept: $L = 12$ cm
- Intermediate intercept: $W = 8$ cm
- Shortest intercept: $T = 7$ cm

**Step 2: Apply the sphericity formula.**

$$\Phi = \dfrac{(L \cdot W \cdot T)^{1/3}}{L}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\Phi = \dfrac{(12 \times 8 \times 7)^{1/3}}{12}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the product under the cube root.**

$$12 \times 8 \times 7 = 672 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 5: Take the cube root.**

$$672^{1/3} = 8.758 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 6: Divide by L.**

$$\Phi = \dfrac{8.758}{12} = 0.730$$

**Step 7: Interpret the result.**

$$\Phi = 0.730 \rightarrow \text{Moderately spherical}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** A mango with $\Phi = 0.73$ will not roll smoothly on a flat sorting conveyor. The **angle of repose** will be **higher** than for a perfect sphere, requiring adjustment in hopper inclination (~20–30° vs ~15° for spheres) to ensure gravity flow.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Color Change ($\Delta E$) in Heat-Processed Tomato Puree

**Problem:** Fresh tomato puree has CIELAB values $L_1^* = 45.2$, $a_1^* = 28.5$, $b_1^* = 22.1$. After thermal sterilization, the values change to $L_2^* = 38.6$, $a_2^* = 22.4$, $b_2^* = 18.3$. Calculate the total color difference $\Delta E$ and assess acceptability.

**Step 1: Recall the formula.**

$$\Delta E = \sqrt{(L_2^* - L_1^*)^2 + (a_2^* - a_1^*)^2 + (b_2^* - b_1^*)^2}$$

**Step 2: Compute individual differences.**

$$\Delta L^* = L_2^* - L_1^* = 38.6 - 45.2 = -6.6$$

$$\Delta a^* = a_2^* - a_1^* = 22.4 - 28.5 = -6.1$$

$$\Delta b^* = b_2^* - b_1^* = 18.3 - 22.1 = -3.8$$

**Step 3: Square each difference.**

$$(\Delta L^*)^2 = (-6.6)^2 = 43.56$$

$$(\Delta a^*)^2 = (-6.1)^2 = 37.21$$

$$(\Delta b^*)^2 = (-3.8)^2 = 14.44$$

**Step 4: Sum the squares.**

$$\Sigma = 43.56 + 37.21 + 14.44 = 95.21$$

**Step 5: Take the square root.**

$$\Delta E = \sqrt{95.21} = 9.76$$

**Step 6: Apply the acceptance criteria.**

| $\Delta E$ Range | Perceived Difference |
|---|---|
| 0 – 1 | Not perceptible |
| 1 – 2 | Barely perceptible |
| 2 – 3.5 | Perceptible on close inspection |
| 3.5 – 5 | Clearly perceptible |
| **5 – 10** | **Greatly perceptible** (consumer noticeable) |
| \> 10 | Strong colour change (objectionable) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** $\Delta E = 9.76$ is *greatly perceptible*. The heat process has caused substantial **lycopene isomerization** and **Maillard browning**. Process optimization (lower temperature, shorter time) is recommended.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — TSS/Acid Ratio for Mango Pulp

**Problem:** A batch of 'Alphonso' mango pulp has TSS = 16.0 °Brix and titratable acidity = 0.40 % (as citric acid). Determine the TSS/Acid ratio and comment on suitability for pulp processing.

**Step 1: Recall the formula.**

$$\text{TSS/Acid Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{TSS (\°Brix)}}{\text{TA (\%)}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{16.0}{0.40} = 40$$

**Step 3: Compare with industry standard.**

The industry standard for *'Alphonso' mango pulp* is **TSS/Acid = 30–45** for acceptable organoleptic quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Ratio = 40 → **Within acceptable range**. The pulp is suitable for processing into nectar, ready-to-serve beverages, or concentrate (after thermal evaporation). If ratio > 50, pulp is "flat" (lacks acidity); if < 25, pulp is excessively sour.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Porosity and Bulk Density of Potato Strips

**Problem:** A sample of potato strips has a bulk mass of 1.20 kg occupying a bulk volume of 2.5 L. The same mass of intact potato tissue, when ground and measured in a pycnometer, has a true density of 1075 kg/m³. Calculate the bulk density and porosity.

**Step 1: Compute bulk density.**

$$\rho_b = \dfrac{m_{bulk}}{V_{bulk}} = \dfrac{1.20 \text{ kg}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3} = 480 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Apply the porosity formula.**

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t} = 1 - \dfrac{480}{1075}$$

**Step 3: Compute.**

$$\varepsilon = 1 - 0.4465 = 0.5535$$

$$\varepsilon = 55.35\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** With 55.35 % porosity, potato strips will dry **efficiently** (high void volume → high air contact). However, such porosity also means the product is **fragile** and prone to breakage during conveying — gentle handling systems are required.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Growing Degree Day (GDD) for Grape Maturity

**Problem:** During a 90-day growth period of a 'Thompson Seedless' grape vine, the average daily max temperature is 28 °C and average min is 14 °C. The base temperature for grapes is 10 °C. Calculate the cumulative GDD.

**Step 1: Recall the formula.**

$$GDD_{daily} = \left( \dfrac{T_{max} + T_{min}}{2} \right) - T_{base}$$

**Step 2: Compute daily GDD.**

$$GDD_{daily} = \left( \dfrac{28 + 14}{2} \right) - 10 = 21 - 10 = 11 \text{ °C-day}$$

**Step 3: Multiply by the number of days.**

$$GDD_{cumulative} = 11 \times 90 = 990 \text{ °C-day}$$

**Step 4: Compare with the standard.**

For wine grapes, harvest is recommended at **GDD ≈ 1100–1400** °C-day. A cumulative GDD of 990 indicates the grapes are **not yet fully mature** for wine production.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** GDD helps processors **schedule harvest**, **predict labor demand**, and **plan cold storage capacity** in advance.

**Define sphericity. How is it calculated for a fruit with three principal dimensions L, W, T? Why is it important in food processing equipment design?**

>
> **Definition (1 mark):** Sphericity ($\Phi$) is the ratio of the surface area of a sphere with the same volume as the produce to the actual surface area of the produce. It is a dimensionless number that quantifies how closely a shape resembles a perfect sphere ($\Phi = 1$ for a sphere, $\Phi < 1$ for elongated/irregular produce).

> $$\Phi = \dfrac{(L \cdot W \cdot T)^{1/3}}{L}$$
>
> where $L$, $W$, $T$ are the longest, intermediate, and shortest principal dimensions (cm or mm).
>
> **Importance (1 mark):** Sphericity governs the **angle of repose** in hoppers, the **drag coefficient** in air/water sorting, the design of **grading screens** and **packaging trays**, and the **heat transfer uniformity** during thermal processing. A produce with low sphericity requires gentler handling and modified equipment geometry.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, ESE Module 2]`

**Q.A.(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **physical characteristics of fruits and vegetables**. Discuss the methods used to measure **size, shape, density, and porosity**, with suitable formulas and engineering significance.

**Q.A.(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Magness-Taylor (MT) firmness test** and the **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)**. Compare both methods and list the textural parameters obtained from a TPA test.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Module 2]`

**Q.B.(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **structure and biochemical composition of the plant cell wall**. Explain the **pectin degradation pathway** and its role in fruit softening, with a note on the enzymes involved.

**Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]** What are **maturity standards**? Classify the various **maturity indices** used in fruits and vegetables. With suitable examples, explain **TSS/Acid ratio** and **Growing Degree Days (GDD)** as maturity indices.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating physiological and horticultural maturity** — examiners deduct up to 2 marks if these are defined as the same concept. Always state the distinction explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong probe diameter in MT test** — the standard is **7.94 mm (5/16 inch)**, not 8 mm or 10 mm. Memorize the exact value.
> 3. **Confusing bulk density with true density** — bulk density includes voids; true density is of the solid alone. Examiners will deduct marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 4. **In TPA, omitting parameters** — full marks require all **7** parameters (Hardness, Cohesiveness, Adhesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, Chewiness, Resilience).
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — always specify units (N, mm, kg/m³, °Brix, %).
> 6. **Pectin pathway missing enzymes** — list **PME, PG, and pectate lyase** explicitly.
> 7. **GDD without base temperature** — never write the GDD formula without specifying the **base temperature ($T_{base}$)**; different crops have different base temperatures.
> 8. **Skipping the ripening physiology** — for any question on maturity standards in climacteric fruits, always mention **ethylene, ACC, ACC synthase, ACC oxidase, and the respiratory climacteric peak**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Master these before the KTU exam:**

### 🔹 Physical Characteristics
- **Size:** L × W × T in cm or mm; measured by **vernier caliper** or **image analysis**.
- **Shape indices:** Aspect Ratio (AR = L/W); Sphericity $\Phi = (LWT)^{1/3}/L$.
- **Mass (m):** in **g or kg**; **Volume (V):** by water displacement.
- **Bulk density** ($\rho_b = m/V_{bulk}$); **True density** ($\rho_t = m/V_{particle}$).
- **Porosity** $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_t$.
- **Specific Gravity** $SG = \rho_{produce}/\rho_{water\ at\ 4°C}$.

### 🔹 Optical (Color) Characteristics
- **CIELAB system:** $L^*, a^*, b^*$.
- **Hue angle** $h^\circ = \tan^{-1}(b^*/a^*)$; **Chroma** $C^* = \sqrt{a^{*2}+b^{*2}}$.
- **Total color difference** $\Delta E = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2+(\Delta a^*)^2+(\Delta b^*)^2}$.
- $\Delta E > 5$ = *greatly perceptible*; $\Delta E > 10$ = *objectionable*.

### 🔹 Textural Characteristics
- **Magness-Taylor probe diameter = 7.94 mm (5/16 inch).**
- **TPA yields 7 parameters:** Hardness, Cohesiveness, Adhesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, Chewiness, Resilience.
- **Gumminess = H × C**; **Chewiness = H × C × S**.
- Plant tissues are **viscoelastic** (Maxwell / Kelvin–Voigt models).
- **Defects:** Mealiness, Woolliness, Lignification, Hard-core.

### 🔹 Structure & Composition
- **Tissues:** Parenchyma, Collenchyma, Sclerenchyma.
- **Cell wall:** Cellulose + Hemicellulose + Pectin + Lignin + Proteins.
- **Pectin pathway:** Protopectin → Pectin → Pectic Acid (PME + PG + Pectate Lyase).
- **Composition (fresh wt):** Water 70–95 %, Carbohydrates 2–25 %, Proteins 0.5–5 %, Lipids 0.1–1 %, Acids 0.2–2 %, Ash 0.3–2 %.

### 🔹 Maturity Standards
- **Physiological maturity** = biological end-point; **Horticultural maturity** = commercial end-point.
- **Climacteric fruits** ripen *after* harvest (mango, banana, tomato, apple, papaya).
- **Non-climacteric fruits** ripen *only* on the plant (citrus, grape, strawberry, pineapple).
- **Ethylene pathway:** Methionine → ACC (via ACC synthase) → Ethylene (via ACC oxidase).
- **Maturity indices:** Physical, Chemical, Physiological, Calculated, Subjective.
- **TSS/Acid ratio** is the master flavor index; **GDD** is the master heat-unit predictor.
- **Industry standard maturity for mango pulp:** TSS ≥ 14 °Brix; SG = 1.00–1.02; **for Alphonso** TSS/Acid = 30–45.
- **Industrial ethylene dose for ripening rooms:** 100–150 ppm for 24–48 h at 18–22 °C, 90–95 % RH.
- **1-MCP** blocks ethylene receptors; used to extend post-harvest life of climacteric fruits.
